segments.s417-g17: دلم is the possessed noun 'دل' + the 1st-person enclitic ('-م') = 'my heart'; گشت is the past of گشتن meaning 'became/turned' (1st clause: 'my heart turned'). زان کار is a literary prepositional phrase 'from/ because of that affair' (زان = 'from that'); چون نوبهار is a simile 'like the new spring' (چون = 'like', نوبهار = 'new spring'). Word order is literary: possessor enclitic attached to دل, verb گشت follows, and the cause and simile follow for poetic effect.
segments.s417-g18: هم ... هم ... is the correlative pair 'both ... and ...'; each از marks the source/target ('from'/'with respect to') and names follow. The phrase as a whole means 'toward/both in regard to Rostam and Esfandiyar.' The order (هم از X و هم از Y) is a common poetic way to express 'both X and Y.'
segments.s417-g19: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when/whenever'. در کارتان is 'in/into your affairs' (کار + plural/possessive -تان). باز کردم is the compound open + do: باز کردن + کردم = 'I opened' (past, 1st person); نگاه is the object 'gaze'/'look'. The verb-final/compound ordering is literary: 'when I opened a gaze into your affairs.'
segments.s417-g20: ببندد is the classical 3rd-person imperfective/present form (literary habitual: 'it closes' / 'it keeps closing'); همی is a classical particle reinforcing continuous or habitual action ('ever/always'). بر خرد = 'upon/against wisdom' (خرد = 'wisdom'); دیو راه is a poetic word-order for 'راهِ دیو' ('the demon's road' or 'the road of the demon') with the ezāfe omitted or inverted for meter. Together: 'the demon always closes the road to wisdom.'
segments.s417-g21: تو is the 2nd-person subject 'you'. آگاهی is a noun 'awareness/knowledge' predicated of تو ('you are aware'). از کار دین و خرد is a prepositional phrase 'of the affairs/matters of religion and wisdom' (از introduces the domain; دین و خرد are coordinated nouns). This is a taut, literary way to say 'you know/conscious of religious and wise matters.'
segments.s417-g22: روانت = روان + enclitic ت 'your soul/spirit' as the subject. همیشه 'always' modifies the predicate. خرد پرورد literally 'nurtures/fosters wisdom': پرورد is a literary present (3rd person) form meaning 'nurtures' and here carries an optative/blessing sense ('may it nurture' / 'may your soul always foster wisdom') common in classical Persian. Word order places object (خرد) before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s417-g23: بپرهیز is an imperative meaning 'refrain' or 'beware'; و = 'and'. با جان ستیزه مکن is a negative command: با جان 'with life' (جان = 'life') and ستیزه مکن = 'do not make/engage in strife' (مکن is negative imperative 'do not'). The clause warns against making war/strife with life (i.e., do not endanger or contend with your life).
segments.s417-g24: نیوشنده باش is a formal imperative: نیوشنده 'listener' (agent noun) + باش 'be' = 'be a listener' or 'listen.' از برادر سخن = 'to/for your brother's speech' (از introduces the source/object: 'listen to the brother's words'). The order is straightforward imperative advice: 'Be a listener to your brother's words.'
segments.s417-g57: ازان (a poetic compound meaning “of that / about that”) precedes the noun, marking the referent; مردی (man) is the direct object of یاد کردن. خود emphasises the subject as ‘himself’. همی is the older, emphatic imperfective prefix (‘used to / would / still’) attached here before the verb phrase; یاد کرد is the verb phrase ‘remembered’ (یاد as noun + کردن), so the literal order is “of-that man himself still remembered (he did)”.
segments.s417-g58: به یاد شهنشاه is a prepositional phrase ‘in/for remembrance of the Shah’ (به + یاد + شهنشاه). جامی is an indefinite accusative ‘a goblet/cup’ and بخورد is the classical/pre-modern form of the verb خوردن with the prefix ب (here used as the narrative/habitual or simple past sense in epic style), so the clause reads ‘(he) drank a cup in memory of the king.’
segments.s417-g59: همی بود is the emphatic/habitual imperfective past-style construction (‘was / remained’); the verb بودن follows the adverbial همی here. Word order is subject-last poetic order: رستم (subject) appears after the verb phrase, then به ایوان خویش (‘in his porch’) gives locative information. خویش is split here into خوی + ش (possessive enclitic); the clitic ش marks ‘his’.
segments.s417-g60: ز خوردن is a dependent infinitival phrase with the poetic preposition ز = ‘from / by’ indicating the action being avoided (‘from eating’). نگه داشت is a compound verb (نگه داشتن) meaning ‘to keep/observe’ (here ‘kept’ / ‘refrained to keep’); پیمان خویش (‘his pledge’) is the object, where خوی + ش again yields ‘his own pledge’. The verbal sequence shows purpose/restriction: he refrained from eating in order to keep his pledge.
segments.s417-g61: چو چندی برآمد is a temporal clause introduced by چو = ‘when’; چندی (‘for a while’) + برآمد (‘arose/elapsed’) together mean ‘after some time had passed’. نیامد کسی places the negated intransitive verb before the pronoun/indefinite to stress that ‘no one came’ (classical word order allows the verb before the subject).
segments.s417-g62: نگه کرد is the light-verb construction (نگه + کرد) meaning ‘kept watch / looked’. The agent رستم follows the verb (poetic order). به ره is the locative phrase ‘toward/along the road’ and بر بسی is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘for a long time/much’ (بسی = ‘much’, with بر functioning to form the duration/intensity).
segments.s417-g63: چو هنگام نان خوردن اندر گذشت is a temporal subordinate clause: چو = ‘when’, هنگام نان خوردن = ‘the time of eating bread’ (an n+gerund construction), اندر = ‘within/at’, and گذشت = ‘passed’. The verb گذشت closes the clause, giving ‘when the time to eat had passed.’
segments.s417-g64: ز introduces the source (‘from’): ز مغز دلیر = ‘from the brave one’s core/brain’ (مغز here ‘core/brain’). آب برتر گذشت uses گذشت (‘passed / flowed’) with آب (‘water’) as subject; برتر here has a comparative/positional force (‘upward/over’ in classical-poetic diction) so the literal sense is ‘water passed upward/from the core’ — a poetic way to say water welled up (tears or moisture).
segments.s417-g41: This clause lists contrasting types of people. یکی (one/someone) is an indefinite subject placed before the noun it qualifies; بزم (feast/banquet) is the object of جوید. جوید is the classical present-tense/attributive form of جستن/جویدن meaning “seeks.” The second نیم‌بند repeat یکی introduces the next coordinated clause (elliptically repeating جوید): رزم (battle) and کین (vengeance/hatred) are the coordinated objects. Word order is Subject–Object–Verb for the first unit (typical in older Persian poetry), with the verb جوید marking the action that also implicitly applies to the second one.
segments.s417-g42: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (gaze/watch) + کن (do!) = “watch!” or “look!” The conjunction که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that'), and تا here has the purposive/observational sense 'in order to / so as to' (introducing the aim of watching). کیست is the copular question form 'who is (it),' with ست the classical copula. با آفرین is an adverbial phrase meaning 'with praise/acclaim' (آفرین = acclamation). Together the clause means 'watch so as to see who it is that comes/appears with acclaim.'
segments.s417-g43: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so.' داد is the past tense of دادن 'gave' and here governs its object پاسخ 'answer' (verb before object is common in poetic prose). ورا is the enclitic indirect object 'to him.' نامدار is a postposed nominative/appositive meaning 'the famed/renowned (one)' — poetic inversion places the epithet after the verb phrase.
segments.s417-g44: که‌گر is the fused conditional particle meaning 'that if' (که + گر). من is the explicit first-person subject 'I.' بپیچم is the present/subjunctive form of پیچیدن with the prefix بـ (classical present-subjunctive morphology) meaning 'I should turn/twist' or 'were to turn'; it marks a hypothetical action. سر از شهریار literally 'head from the king' is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'turn away from / break allegiance with the king' (سر = head, از = from, شهریار = the king). The clause is a conditional subordinate clause: 'if I were to turn my head away from the king.'
segments.s417-g45: بدین گیتی‌اندر functions as a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in this world' (بدین = 'by/with this' / 'in this', گیتی‌اندر = 'in the world'). نکوهش بود is the predicate 'was censure/reproach' (بود = past copula 'was'). The whole clause says that in this world he was under blame or reproach. Note classical poetic word-order and contraction of prepositional material.
segments.s417-g46: همان is a demonstrative/adjectival pronoun 'that/same' referring back to the person; پیش یزدان is a locative phrase 'before/in the presence of God' (پیش = before/in the presence of, یزدان = God). پژوهش بود literally 'was an inquiry' or 'was under investigation' (پژوهش = investigation/examination, بود = 'was'). The clause contrasts worldly blame with divine scrutiny: before God he faced investigation.
segments.s417-g47: دو گیتی 'the two worlds' (a common Persian idiom meaning 'both worlds' or 'the whole world/this life and the next') is fronted for emphasis. به رستم 'to Rostam' is the indirect object/recipient. نخواهم فروخت is the negative future/conditional construction: ن + خواهم (classical auxiliary expressing volition/future) + فروخت (infinitive/past stem) = 'I will not sell' or 'I would not sell.' The clause asserts the speaker's refusal to give up everything even for Rostam.
segments.s417-g48: کسی here with the negative verb means 'no one' (in Persian, کسی + negated verb = 'no one ...'). چشم دین را is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the eye of religion' with را marking it as definite object/topicalized. به سوزن (literally 'to/onto a needle') and ندوخت is the past negative of دوختن ('did not sew'); the negative prefix ن- attaches to the past form, yielding 'did not sew/stitched not.' The line uses a vivid image — 'no one sewed religion's eye to a needle' — to make a moral/figurative point.
segments.s417-g49: بدو گفت is an inversion meaning “he said to him” (بدو = 'to him', گفته implied); the following phrase 'هر چیز کامد ز پند' is an elliptical relative clause: literally 'every thing that comes from advice' (کامد is an archaic present 'comes', and ز = 'from'). The relative particle که is omitted, common in poetry.
segments.s417-g50: This is the predicative phrase: 'تن پاک و جان ترا سودمند' = 'beneficial to your pure body and soul.' تن پاک = 'pure body'; جان ترا = 'your soul' (ت is the 2nd-person suffix and را follows as the emphatic/definite object marker, an older form equivalent to 'ترا' = 'to you'); سودمند is the predicate adjective 'beneficial'.
segments.s417-g51: همه گفتم literally 'I said to all' (همه = everyone; گفتم = 'I said' — the provided senses list lacked the verbal sense, so noted accordingly). اکنون = 'now'; بهی برگزین is an imperative 'choose the best' (بهی = 'the good/best', برگزین = 'choose!'). Word order places the addressed group before the verb, typical in narrative.
segments.s417-g52: دل شهریاران = 'the hearts of the kings' (a genitive/construct: noun + plural possessive noun). نیازد is a third-person present/epistolary-form of نیازیدن 'to need/long for' (here 'needs'); به کین = 'for vengeance' with به marking the object of نیازیدن. The clause states a general disposition: 'the hearts of kings need vengeance.'
segments.s417-g53: سپهبد ز خوالیگران خواست خوان = 'the spahbed asked the khwāligran for a reciter.' ز marks source/agent ('from' or 'of'); خواست is past 'requested/asked'; خوان here is the person who reads/sings (reciter) rather than 'banquet' — the poem uses خوان in that sense.
segments.s417-g54: کسی را نفرمود کو را بخوان = 'he did not tell anyone whom to call.' کسی را is the indirect object 'to anyone' (را marks the object); نفرمود is the negative past of فرمودن ('did not tell/say') — the provided sense list did not include the negative-past reading; 'کو را بخوان' is a subordinate interrogative-like phrase 'whom to call' (کو functions here as 'who/whom' in older usage, and را marks the object of بخوان).
segments.s417-g55: چو نان خوردِه شد = a temporal subordinate clause 'when the bread had been eaten' (چو = 'when'; خورده شد = perfect/passive 'was eaten' or 'had been eaten', where شد functions as auxiliary). جام می‌برگرفت is an imperfect/habitual past 'he would take up the cup' (می‌برگرفت shows repeated or customary past action).
segments.s417-g56: ز رویین دژ = 'from the iron fortress' (ز = 'from', رویین = 'iron‑hard/iron', دژ = 'fortress'); آنگه = 'then'; سخن درگرفت = 'he began to speak' or 'the speech seized hold' (درگرفت used idiomatically to mean 'began'). The verb درگرفت is past third-person, and the prepositional phrase precedes it in typical poetic order.
segments.s417-g65: بخندید is a third-person past tense verb 'he laughed.' و simply links clauses ('and'). گفت is a reported-speech verb 'said' (here followed by the quoted/imperative content). ای برادر is a vocative phrase ('O brother') addressing someone; تو is the explicit second-person subject 'you'; خوان is a noun 'banquet/table' placed before the imperatives that follow (object of the commands). Note the verb 'گفت' introduces what follows as direct speech/command.
segments.s417-g66: بیارای is an imperative/jussive command from آراستن 'prepare / adorn the table' addressed to 'تو'. و connects two imperatives ('and'). آزادگان را is the definite object ('the free/nobles') marked by را; بخوان is a second-person imperative 'call/summon/recite' directed at the addressee. The object marker را attaches to آزادگان to mark them as the direct object of بخوان.
segments.s417-g67: گرینست is a third-person past form meaning 'he/it wept' or 'was given to weeping' and here predicates the following noun phrase. آیین اسفندیار is a genitive construction 'the custom/ritual of Esfandiyar' (آیین governed by the name اسفندیار). The verb precedes the noun phrase for poetic effect: 'the custom of Esfandiyar was to weep.'
segments.s417-g68: تو is the vocative/second-person pronoun 'you.' آیین این نامور is 'the custom of this renowned [one]' (این + نامور modifying آیین). یاد دار is a verbal phrase 'keep in memory / remember' where یاد is 'remembrance' and دار is the verb form meaning 'hold/keep' (an older/poetic usage here). The clause is an imperative/instruction: 'you keep/remember this famed custom.'
segments.s417-g69: بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb meaning 'he ordered/commanded.' تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that / that'), followed by a subjunctive verb. رخش را is the object 'his steed' marked by را, زین is 'saddle,' and کنند is the present-subjunctive/plural 'they do/make' — together 'he ordered that they saddle his steed.'
segments.s417-g70: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic 'that same / that very.' زین repeats 'saddle' as the item to be worked on. به آرایش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with adornment / in decoration' (به + آرایش), and چین کنند is a subjunctive plural 'they arrange / they pleat / they set in folds' used here to mean 'they decorate/arrange' the saddle. The clause continues the order: 'and let them ornament that same saddle.'
segments.s417-g71: شوم here is an optative/first-person form ('let me be' / a formulaic 'let me'), used with باز 'again' plus گویم to form 'let me speak/tell again.' باز is an adverb 'again.' گویم is the first-person present/subjunctive 'I say / I will say' and is followed by the preposition به 'to' introducing the addressee اسفندیار. The whole phrase is an introductory formula: 'Let me again tell Esfandiyar...'.
segments.s417-g72: کجا is the interrogative adverb 'where.' کار ما را is 'our affair' with ما (our) and the object-marker را. گرفتست is a past/perfect verb 'was taken / was seized / was overcome' and خوار is a predicate adjective 'lowly / humiliated / despised' describing the state into which the affair has fallen. Word order places the verb before the adjective: 'where has our affair been taken low.'
segments.s417-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning "when" introducing a subordinate time clause. The proper name رستم follows it as the clause subject. برفت is an archaic/poetic past-form meaning "went away/left" (here treated as the simple past), and از لب هیرمند is a prepositional phrase (= "from the bank of Helmand") with از marking origin and لب used in the sense of "brink/bank." Word order is somewhat inverted as is common in epic poetry: conjunction + subject + verbal phrase + prepositional complement.
segments.s417-g2: پراندیشه is a compound adjective (پر‑ + اندیشه) meaning "full of thought/pensive" and functions as the predicative adjective. شد is the past of شدن "to become," so the phrase means "became pensive." نامدار بلند is an epithet ("renowned, lofty") that identifies the subject (Rostam) and appears after the predicate for poetic emphasis.
segments.s417-g3: پشوتن is the proper name (subject). که introduces a relative/nominal clause "who/that..." and here links the name to the descriptive phrase. بد modifies رهنمای and means "bad/ill-"; شاه را is the king marked by را (object/affected party), and رهنمای is the noun "guide/adviser." The clause therefore describes Peshotan as "a bad guide to the king."
segments.s417-g4: بیامد is the simple past "came." هم‌انگه is an adverb meaning "at that very moment/straightaway," modifying the verb. به + پرده سرای is a locative/directional phrase "into/to the curtained chamber" with به marking direction and پرده ("curtain") qualifying the chamber; the compound پرده‌سرا (curtained-room) is expressed poetically here. Note: سرای as given in the word-list has unrelated senses; here it functions as a locative noun meaning "chamber/house."
segments.s417-g5: چنین گفت is a common formulaic phrase meaning "thus said" (چنین = "thus/so", گفت = past "said"). Word order here is inverted for emphasis: the indirect object با او ("to him") appears before the subject, and یل اسفندیار ("the hero Esfandiyar") names the speaker. In natural English this becomes "Esfandiyar the hero said to him thus."
segments.s417-g6: که introduces the content of what was said (a subordinate/that-clause). کاری گرفتیم is idiomatic: کاری ("a task/affair") + گرفتیم (1st pl. past "we took/undertook"), i.e. "we have undertaken a task." دشخوار خوار appears as a condensed object/description of that task — poetically stating the aim or nature of the task (literally two epithets), something like "to abase/despise the fearsome/destroyer-of-foes." Epic Persian often uses such terse adjectival strings to specify purpose or result.
segments.s417-g7: به ایوان رستم is a locative phrase "at/to Rostam's iwan/porch." مرا کار نیست is an existential/dative construction meaning literally "to me there is no affair" → "I have no business/concern." In this construction مرا (the dative/experiencer) precedes the nominal subject کار and the copula نیست expresses absence ("there is not").
segments.s417-g8: ورا (to him) + نزد من (in my presence/before me) sets the person and location; نیز (= "also") links this clause to the previous negation. دیدار نیست is again an existential negation: "there is no meeting/visit." Together: "nor is there any meeting with him in my presence." Word order places the indirect object (ورا) and locative (نزد من) before the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s417-g25: شنیدم is 1st-person past 'I heard.' The clause همه هرچ رستم بگفت is a quantified free-relative object: همه 'all/everyone' + هرچ 'whatever' introduces the content clause رستم بگفت ('that Rostam said'). بگفت is past third-person 'he said.' Word order places the verb شنیدم before its object clause.
segments.s417-g26: بزرگیش is the possessed abstract 'his greatness' (بزرگ + ـیش). با مردمی 'with/by the people' gives the comparison/partner, and بود (past of بودن) is the copula. جفت here functions as predicate-noun meaning 'a match/equal' (archaic poetic usage). The phrase therefore says his greatness 'was a match with/by the people.'
segments.s417-g27: نساید is an archaic impersonal/preterite-like negative meaning 'it does not (or did not) befit / is not fitting' (here negating the propriety). دو پای ورا بند تو is the object/infinitival idea 'to bind his two feet (by you)' — دو پای 'two feet', ورا = 'to him', بند = 'fetter/bond', تو = 'you' (agent). Word order places the evaluative verb before the action phrase.
segments.s417-g28: نیاید is negative/modal here meaning something like 'he will not / should not come.' سبک modifies the manner 'lightly' (سبک = light/frivolous), سوی = 'toward', پیوند = 'bond/union', تو = 'you.' Together: 'he will not lightly come toward your bond' — an adverbial clause stating he will not readily submit.
segments.s417-g29: This is a string of epithets/apposition describing the subject: سوار 'rider/horseman', جهان 'world' modifying پور (here read as the element 'son/descendant'), and دستان + سام read together as a patronymic/name sequence (Sam). The sequence lists titles/relations of the person being mentioned.
segments.s417-g30: به بازی = 'by/with a play/trick' (بازی here 'trick/ploy'). سراندر is an adverbial/locative compound meaning 'into the midst/within' (i.e., into). نیارد is the verbal core meaning 'does not bring / will not bring' (archaic spelling/formation), and به دام = 'into the trap/snare.' The whole clause says he will not bring (someone) into the trap by a trick.
segments.s417-g31: چنو is a demonstrative/adverb meaning 'such' or 'like this.' پهلوانی is the abstract 'heroism/heroic deed.' ز introduces source/association 'from/among,' and گردنکشان ('neck-pullers') is a poetic term for rebellious/proud fighters. Together: 'such heroism among the neck-pullers.'
segments.s417-g32: ندادست is an archaic past 'did not give' (negated perfective/past), دانا 'the wise one' is the subject/agent, and به گیتی نشان is a prepositional phrase 'as a sign/token to the world' (به = to/for, گیتی = world, نشان = sign/token). Literal order is verb + subject + indirect object phrase, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s417-g33: چگونه توان کرد is a rhetorical question: چگونه (how) + توان (ability auxiliary) + کرد (the full verb 'do/make'). توان here is a modal expressing possibility and کرد is the main verb that completes that modality. پایش (پای + ش, ‘his foot’) is the object/thing being acted on and به بند (به + بند, ‘into fetters’) is the prepositional complement: literally ‘how can one do (i.e. put) his foot into fetters.’ Word order is typical classical Persian: question word → modal → verb, with the object phrase following.
segments.s417-g34: مگوی is the negative imperative/prohibitive ‘do not say’ (مگو + negative suffix). The clause آنکه هرگز نیاید پسند is the content clause quoted or object of مگوی: آنکه ‘he/who that’, هرگز ‘never’, نیاید (ن + آید) is a modal/subjunctive present used here as ‘does/shall not come (to be)’ and پسند is the predicate noun ‘approval/being pleasing’ — the verb is postposed in classical order (نیاید precedes پسند). Altogether: “Do not say: ‘he who will never be pleasing/acceptable.’”
segments.s417-g35: سخنهای is the plural construct ‘words/sayings’ (سخن + -ها/-های). ناخوب و نادلپذیر are two coordinating adjectives modifying سخنهای: ناخوب ‘bad’ and نادلپذیر ‘unpleasant/unacceptable.’ The adjectives precede or follow nouns in Persian; here they follow the noun, forming a descriptive phrase ‘ugly and unpleasant words.’
segments.s417-g36: This clause gives the agent and the judgment: سزدگر is an older/formal word meaning roughly ‘one who judges it fitting’ or a particle meaning ‘it is fitting’ qualifying the verb. نگوید is a negative jussive/indicative meaning ‘(he) should not/does not say’ (here a prescriptive sense). یل شیرگیر is the agent noun phrase ‘the hero (یل), the lion‑catcher (شیرگیر)’ — Persian often places the predicate (سزدگر نگوید) before or around the subject for poetic style; the copula is omitted. Reading: ‘A lion‑catcher hero ought not to say (such things).’
segments.s417-g37: بترسم is a first‑person present/subjunctive expression ‘I fear’ (ب‑ prefix + ترسم). که introduces the subordinate clause ‘that…’. این کار is the subject ‘this affair/act’ and گردد (from گشت/گردیدن) is the subjunctive/optative ‘may become’ or ‘become’ used after verbs of fearing; دراز is the predicate adjective ‘long/prolonged.’ The structure is: ‘I fear that this affair become long.’
segments.s417-g38: به زشتی is an adverbial phrase: به + زشتی = ‘into/with ugliness’ (marking the manner/result). میان دو گردن‌فراز is a locative/prepositional phrase ‘between two گردن‌فراز’ (گردن‌فراز = ‘proud/boastful necks’, i.e. proud persons). Together they modify how the matter will become دراز: ‘become prolonged into ugliness between two proud men.’
segments.s417-g39: This is a string of qualities with an implicit copula: بزرگی = ‘greatness’ (a noun standing as a quality), و links the next phrase, and از شاه داناتری is a comparative phrase where از functions as the comparative marker ‘than’ (lit. ‘from the king’) and داناتری is the comparative adjective ‘more learned/wiser.’ The copula (is/are) is omitted as is normal in Persian adjectival lists: ‘(He has) greatness, and (is) wiser than the king.’
segments.s417-g40: به مردی و گردی تواناتری uses به to mark the domain/quality ‘in’ (به مردی = ‘in manliness’). The second noun گردی stands in parallel with مردی and both are modified by the comparative adjective تواناتری (‘more able/stronger’). The copula is again omitted: ‘(He is) more manly and more capable (in prowess).’ Note that in classical Persian such parallel noun phrases often share a single comparative adjective at the end.
segments.s417-g9: همان‌گر is a conditional preform (literally 'if likewise') setting up a subordinate clause; نیاید is the negative present/subjunctive form of آمدن used here as a conditional/present ('does not come' / 'should not come'). نخوانمش is نخوانم + ـش (the enclitic pronoun 'him/it') — literally 'I will not call/name/read him' (in heroic diction خواندن can mean 'to summon/mention'); نیز at the end is a simple adverb 'also/too' linking this refusal to a previous statement.
segments.s417-g10: گر is the conditional 'if'. از ما یکی is a partitive expression 'one of us' (از = 'of/from', ما = 'we/us', یکی = 'one'). را is the definite/object marker attached to یکی (here marking/focusing that particular person). برآید is a present/subjunctive/optative sense of برآمدن used to mean 'be possible / succeed / avail' ('if one of us is able'). قفیز is an obscure/rare noun (left untranslated here) that functions as the thing/goal related to برآید.
segments.s417-g11: دل زنده places the noun 'دل' ('heart') before the adjective/participial modifier 'زنده' ('alive,living'); از introduces the cause/agent 'from/by the slain' (کشته here is a substantive meaning 'the slain'); بریان شود uses بریان (literally 'roasted/consumed') with the auxiliary شود to mean 'become seared/consumed' (figuratively: tormented or consumed by grief).
segments.s417-g12: سر is 'the head' (here metonymically for the person or mind); از marks cause 'because of / from'; آشناییش is آشنا + possessive suffix ـش ('his/its acquaintance/knowing him') and functions as the cause ('from knowing him' / 'because of their acquaintance'); گریان شود uses the adjective/participial گریان 'weeping' with شود to mean 'become weeping' / 'will weep'.
segments.s417-g13: پشوتن بدو گفت is a straightforward SOV clause: the proper name پشوتن (Peshotan) is the subject, بدو = به + او 'to him' (dative), and گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (classical/simple past). کای is an archaic/vocative particle 'O' addressing someone directly; نامدار is an epithet 'renowned/illustrious' following the vocative.
segments.s417-g14: برادر is a vocative/form of address 'brother' (used here as a familiar address). که introduces a relative/interrogative clause 'who/that' and یابد is the present/subjunctive/optative form of یافتن ('find, attain') used idiomatically as 'who could find/match'. چو is the comparative 'like/as', followed by the proper name اسفندیار.
segments.s417-g15: به یزدان is an oath 'by God' (به used as an adverbial 'by/with' in oaths). که here introduces the subordinate clause 'that/when'; دیدم is the simple past 'I saw/met', شما را uses شما as the object with را marking definiteness/emphasis ('I saw you'), and نخست is an adverb 'at first/initially'.
segments.s417-g16: که again introduces a clause 'that'. یک نامور is 'one/far famed man' (یک = 'one', نامور = 'renowned'); با is the preposition 'with', دگر 'another/other'. کین is the noun 'vengeance/hatred/enmity', and نجست is the past form of جستن ('sought') — here 'searched/searched after' or 'sought' (so: 'sought enmity').

sentences.s417-s9: Because of that affair my heart turned, like the new spring, toward both Rostam and Esfandiyar.
sentences.s417-s10: Whenever I looked into your affairs, the demon always closes the road to wisdom.
sentences.s417-s11: You are aware of matters of religion and wisdom; may your soul always foster wisdom.
sentences.s417-s12: Refrain, and do not contend with life; be a listener to your brother's words.
sentences.s417-s29: He himself remembered that man, and in remembrance of the Shah he drank a cup.
sentences.s417-s30: Rostam remained in his porch; to keep his pledge he refrained from eating.
sentences.s417-s31: When some time had passed and no one came, Rostam watched the road for a long while.
sentences.s417-s32: When the time for eating bread had passed, water welled up from the hero’s core.
sentences.s417-s21: Some seek revelry, some seek battle and vengeance; watch and see who it is that comes with acclaim.
sentences.s417-s22: Thus the renowned one gave him this answer: 'If I were to turn my head away from the king...'
sentences.s417-s23: In this world he was censured; before God he was put to inquiry.
sentences.s417-s24: I will not sell the two worlds to Rostam; no one stitched the eye of religion onto a needle.
sentences.s417-s25: He said to him: anything that comes from counsel is beneficial to your pure body and soul.
sentences.s417-s26: I said to all: now choose the best — the hearts of kings need vengeance.
sentences.s417-s27: The spahbed asked the khwāligran for a reciter; he did not tell anyone whom to summon.
sentences.s417-s28: When the bread had been eaten, he would take up the cup; then from the iron fortress he began to speak.
sentences.s417-s33: He laughed and said, "O brother, you set the table in order and summon the nobles."
sentences.s417-s34: Esfandiyar's custom was to weep; you, keep the memory of this famed practice.
sentences.s417-s35: He commanded that they saddle his steed; let them adorn that very saddle with decoration.
sentences.s417-s36: Let me again tell Esfandiyar where our affair has been brought low.
sentences.s417-s1: When Rostam departed from the bank of the Helmand, the lofty, famed one became thoughtful.
sentences.s417-s2: Peshotan, who had been an ill-guide to the king, came at that moment into the curtained chamber.
sentences.s417-s3: The hero Esfandiyar said to him thus: "We have undertaken a task to abase the dread foe."
sentences.s417-s4: I have no business at Rostam's portico, nor do I have any meeting with him.
sentences.s417-s13: I heard everything Rostam said; his greatness was matched among the people.
sentences.s417-s14: It does not befit you to bind his two feet; he will not lightly come toward your bond.
sentences.s417-s15: Sam, the world's rider and son of Dastan, will not, by a trick, bring him into a snare.
sentences.s417-s16: Such feats of heroism among the neck-pullers the wise did not give as a token to the world.
sentences.s417-s17: How can one put his foot in fetters? Do not say, “the one who will never be liked.”
sentences.s417-s18: A lion‑catching hero should not utter ugly and unpleasant words.
sentences.s417-s19: I fear that this matter may become prolonged into ugliness between two proud necks (i.e. between two proud men).
sentences.s417-s20: Greater in greatness and wiser than the king; more manly and more capable in prowess.
sentences.s417-s5: If that one does not come, I too will not summon him; but if one of us can manage it, (then) qafiz.
sentences.s417-s6: The living heart will be seared by the slain; the head will become full of tears because of knowing him.
sentences.s417-s7: Peshotan said to him, 'O renowned one, brother — who could match Esfandiyar?'
sentences.s417-s8: By God, when I first saw you, I observed that no famed man sought enmity with another.
